items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.correct: Increases red blood cell production, improving oxygen-carrying capacity.
items.why: Students assume all PEDs work the same way. EPO is specifically for endurance - more red cells = more oxygen to working muscles. Anabolic steroids do something different (promote protein synthesis).

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: Elite male road cyclists typically have VO‚āāmax values around:
items.why: Correct answer: 80‚Äď90 mL kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ min‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. ‚Äú30‚Äď40 mL kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ min‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚ÄĚ sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic ‚ÄĒ but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: VO‚āāmax typically declines without training by approximately:
items.why: It's 10% per decade after age 25. ‚Äú5% per year after age 30‚ÄĚ is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If a 25-year-old's VO‚āāmax is 50 mL kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ min‚ĀĽ¬Ļ and declines 10% per decade untrained, what is it at age 55 (mL kg‚ĀĽ¬Ļ min‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)?
items.why: The real figure is 36.5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close ‚ÄĒ anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
